metadata.title:
UNION MEN ALLEGE THEY
WE'RE EucHRED ON CITY
SEWER BIDS.

pages.raw_text:
, Members Of Federal union No.
1924, which yesterday bid Oh city
sewer work and was underbidden
$1,000, are certain that they were
outplayed by combination Of con-
tractors. They claim to have dis
covered that there Is an under
standing among city work contrac-
tors that the successful bidder
should lose nothing in taking the
work at a figure that ordinarily
would mean a loss of money, in Or
der to shut out the union bid. This
could be accomplished by the other
contractors pooling to reimburse
the contractor who does the work
to the extent of protecting him.


The federal bid was figured on
tho actual wages of the men that
would have been employed. 1t
would be an object, obviously, to
the contractors to prevent any bids
being accepted on such basis.


The union contention is borne
out by the fact that the successful
bids yesterday totalled $1O,310,
while the engineers estimate was
$14,00785. At any rate the union
saved the interested property own
ers nearly $4,000 had the bid even
been UP to the estimate. .....


Figured by lots the cost of the
work will be $S5 per lot where it
formerly ran $125 and $3.50. If
the theory Of contractors COM
bine to shut out labor union bids
is correct they will have consid-
cradle margin to make up to the
man who does the jobs.


Federal union officials are by no
means discouraged and are plan
ming to come back into the lists the
next time city work is advertised
for bids. They are figuring on the
Sprague st. trestle fill, which would
give work to about every idle man
j ntown were the city to start it..
The engineers estimate on this is
$4,000, of which it is expected
the W. w. P. Co. and Traction Co.
will pay nearly half.


The union has been forced into
the contracting business by the al
legged discrimination practiced by
contractors against local union men
in favor of the floating class who
have drifted into town to stay for
the winter and are willing to work
for any wage offered until they can
get money enough ahead to get
out again.


The contractors are charged with
double motive in combining
against the union, that of keeping
the work themselves and of also
keeping wages down by being able
to offer employment inducements
to transient labor.


The union members argue that
the price of public improvements
has been boosted to such figures by
contractors working in concert that
the cost has become practically pro-
hibitive to anybody of moderate
means. and they figure that they
can do the public an immense
amount of good by continuing to
bid. even If they never get a con-
tract, as long as this course forces
the bids down to level where the
average man can see his street i'm
proved without the prospect of pow
erty in consequence.
